title: Campfire Math & Practical Measurements
subject: Applied Mathematics & Physics
description: Learn volumetric measurements, weight, fuel ratio calculations, and geometry through campfires and homestead woodpiles.
learningObjectives: Calculate firewood volume in cords and cubic feet.
Understand surface-area-to-volume ratio in tinder vs fuel logs.
Perform liquid volume conversions for water storage (Liters to Gallons).

lessons.title: The Geometry of Tinder, Kindling, and Fuel
lessons.content: Thin twigs have high surface area relative to volume, making them ignite rapidly. Thick logs have low surface area, burning slowly for long-term heat. To build a fire: start with 80% high-surface-area tinder, transition to medium kindling, then add heavy fuel logs.

lessons.title: Measuring Firewood: Cords & Volume
lessons.content: A full cord of wood measures 4 feet high by 4 feet deep by 8 feet long (4' x 4' x 8' = 128 cubic feet). A face cord is 4 feet high by 8 feet long by 16 inches deep (4' x 8' x 1.33' = 42.6 cubic feet).

flashcards.definition: A unit of volume for stacked firewood equal to 128 cubic feet (4 x 4 x 8 ft).
The total outer area of an object exposed to air and heat.
Small dry sticks used to raise temperature to ignite larger fuel logs.

questions.question: How many cubic feet are in a standard full cord of stacked firewood measuring 4 ft x 4 ft x 8 ft?
Why does fine birch bark tinder catch fire faster than a thick oak log?
options: It has a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio

questions.question: If 1 US gallon equals approximately 3.78 Liters, how many Liters are in a 5-gallon water jug?

practicalChallenge.title: Firewood Stack & Volume Audit
practicalChallenge.instruction: Measure a stack of wood using a tape measure (Length x Width x Height in inches). Divide by 1,728 to find total cubic feet!
